source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure Secrets are Not Stored in Cloud Functions Environment Variables by Using Secret Manager

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure Secrets are Not Stored in Cloud Functions Environment Variables by Using Secret Manager
input_row.description: Google Cloud Functions allow you to host serverless code that is executed when an event is triggered, without the requiring the management a host operating system. These functions can also store environment variables to be used by the code that may contain authentication or other information that needs to remain confidential.
input_row.rationale: It is recommended to use the Secret Manager, because environment variables are stored unencrypted, and accessible for all users who have access to the code. Impact: There should be no impact on the Cloud Function. There are minor costs after 10,000 requests a month to the Secret Manager API as well for a high use of other functions. Modifying the Cloud Function to use the Secret Manager may prevent it running to completion.
input_row.audit: Determine if Confidential Information is Stored in your Functions in Cleartext From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project you wish to audit, select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Scroll down to under the heading 'Serverless', then select 'Cloud Functions' 2. Click on a function name from the list 3. Open the Variables tab and you will see both buildEnvironmentVariables and environmentVariables 4. Review the variables whether they are secrets 5. Repeat step 3-5 until all functions are reviewed From Google Cloud CLI 1. To view a list of your cloud functions run gcloud functions list 2. For each cloud function in the list run the following command. gcloud functions describe <function_name> 3. Review the settings of the buildEnvironmentVariables and environmentVariables. Determine if this is data that should not be publicly accessible. Determine if Secret Manager API is 'Enabled' for your Project From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project you wish to audit, select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'APIs & Services' to under the heading 'Serverless', then select 'Enabled APIs & Services' in the menu that opens up. 2. Click the button '+ Enable APIS and Services' 3. In the Search bar, search for 'Secret Manager API' and select it. 4. If it is enabled, the blue box that normally says 'Enable' will instead say 'Manage'. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Within the project you wish to audit, run the following command. gcloud services list 2. If 'Secret Manager API' is in the list, it is enabled.
input_row.remediation: Enable Secret Manager API for your Project From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project you wish to enable, select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'APIs & Services' to under the heading 'Serverless', then select 'Enabled APIs & Services' in the menu that opens up. 2. Click the button '+ Enable APIS and Services' 3. In the Search bar, search for 'Secret Manager API' and select it. 4. Click the blue box that says 'Enable'. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Within the project you wish to enable the API in, run the following command. gcloud services enable Secret Manager API Reviewing Environment Variables That Should Be Migrated to Secret Manager From Google Cloud Console 1. Log in to the Google Cloud Web Portal (https://console.cloud.google.com/) 2. Go to Cloud Functions 3. Click on a function name from the list 4. Click on Edit and review the Runtime environment for variables that should be secrets. Leave this list open for the next step. From Google Cloud CLI 1. To view a list of your cloud functions run gcloud functions list 2. For each cloud function run the following command. gcloud functions describe <function_name> 3. Review the settings of the buildEnvironmentVariables and environmentVariables. Keep this information for the next step. Migrating Environment Variables to Secrets within the Secret Manager From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Secret Manager page in the Cloud Console. 2. On the Secret Manager page, click Create Secret. 3. On the Create secret page, under Name, enter the name of the Environment Variable you are replacing. This will then be the Secret Variable you will reference in your code. 4. You will also need to add a version. This is the actual value of the variable that will be referenced from the code. To add a secret version when creating the initial secret, in the Secret value field, enter the value from the Environment Variable you are replacing. 5. Leave the Regions section unchanged. 6. Click the Create secret button. 7. Repeat for all Environment Variables From Google Cloud CLI 1. Run the following command with the Environment Variable name you are replacing in the <secret-id>. It is most secure to point this command to a file with the Environment Variable value located in it, as if you entered it via command line it would show up in your shell’s command history. gcloud secrets create <secret-id> --data-file="/path/to/file.txt" Granting your Runtime's Service Account Access to Secrets From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project containing your runtime login with account that has the 'roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor' permission. 2. Select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'Security' to under the then select 'Secret Manager' in the menu that opens up. 3. Click the name of a secret listed in this screen. 4. If it is not already open, click Show Info Panel in this screen to open the panel. 5.In the info panel, click Add principal. 6.In the New principals field, enter the service account your function uses for its identity. (If you need help locating or updating your runtime's service account, please see the 'docs/securing/function-identity#runtime_service_account' reference.) 5. In the Select a role dropdown, choose Secret Manager and then Secret Manager Secret Accessor. From Google Cloud CLI As of the time of writing, using Google CLI to list Runtime variables is only in beta. Because this is likely to change we are not including it here. Modifying the Code to use the Secrets in Secret Manager From Google Cloud Console This depends heavily on which language your runtime is in. For the sake of the brevity of this recommendation, please see the '/docs/creating-and-accessing-secrets#access' reference for language specific instructions. From Google Cloud CLI This depends heavily on which language your runtime is in. For the sake of the brevity of this recommendation, please see the' /docs/creating-and-accessing-secrets#access' reference for language specific instructions. Deleting the Insecure Environment Variables Be certain to do this step last. Removing variables from code actively referencing them will prevent it from completing successfully. From Google Cloud Console 1. Select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'Security' then select 'Secret Manager' in the menu that opens up. 2. Click the name of a function. Click Edit. 3. Click Runtime, build and connections settings to expand the advanced configuration options. 4. Click 'Security’. Hover over the secret you want to remove, then click 'Delete'. 5. Click Next. Click Deploy. The latest version of the runtime will now reference the secrets in Secret Manager. From Google Cloud CLI gcloud functions deploy <Function name>--remove-env-vars <env vars> If you need to find the env vars to remove, they are from the step where ‘gcloud functions describe <function_name>’ was run. Default Value: By default Secret Manager is not enabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/configuring/env-var#managing_secrets 2. https://cloud.google.com/secret-manager/docs/overview Additional Information: There are slight additional costs to using the Secret Manager API. Review the documentation to determine your organizations' needs.
input_row.impact: There should be no impact on the Cloud Function. There are minor costs after 10,000 requests a month to the Secret Manager API as well for a high use of other functions. Modifying the Cloud Function to use the Secret Manager may prevent it running to completion.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/configuring/env-var#managing_secrets 2. https://cloud.google.com/secret-manager/docs/overview Additional Information: There are slight additional costs to using the Secret Manager API. Review the documentation to determine your organizations' needs.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- ✓ Can GCP APIs query this property? Yes, environment variables of Cloud Functions and the state of the Secret Manager API can be queried using GCP APIs.
- ✓ Is this a standard GCP service property (encryption, logging, access control, network rules)? Yes, managing and verifying environment variables and secret management integration are standard properties.
- ✓ Does Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK have methods to check this? Yes, `gcloud functions describe` and `gcloud services list` can be used to extract necessary information.
- ✓ Does GCP CLI have commands to retrieve this? Yes, the Google Cloud CLI supports commands to list and describe Cloud Functions, check environment variables, and verify if the Secret Manager API is enabled.
- ✓ Does audit mention Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/GCP CLI commands? Yes, it explicitly mentions using `gcloud functions describe` and `gcloud services list`.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_functions_environment_variables_secrets_managed

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
- Use Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) to list all cloud functions in a project using: `gcloud functions list`.
- For each cloud function, describe its configuration: `gcloud functions describe <function_name>`.
- Check `environmentVariables` and `buildEnvironmentVariables` for the presence of any confidential or secret values.
- Verify if the Secret Manager API is enabled by checking the project's enabled services: `gcloud services list --enabled`.
- Ensure Cloud Functions do not store any secrets directly in environment variables.
- Ensure Secret Manager is used to handle all sensitive information by confirming that required secrets are stored and accessed through Secret Manager.
- Pass Criteria: All secrets are managed through Secret Manager and no sensitive data is stored in unencrypted or publicly accessible environments.
- Fail Criteria: Detection of sensitive data within environment variables or the Secret Manager API is not enabled.
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is *mostly* correct. The presence of environment variables and whether Secret Manager is enabled can be programmatically checked using GCP APIs and `gcloud` commands. However, **detecting whether an environment variable contains a "secret" is not fully automatable**—it requires context or pattern-matching, which is error-prone and may require manual review or advanced DLP (Data Loss Prevention) integration.  
- **GCP APIs/SDK/CLI:**  
  - `gcloud functions list` and `gcloud functions describe` can enumerate and retrieve environment variables.
  - `gcloud services list --enabled` can check if Secret Manager is enabled.
  - However, **verifying that all secrets are managed through Secret Manager and not in environment variables cannot be fully automated** unless you have a deterministic way to identify secrets (e.g., regex for key names or values, which is not foolproof).
- **CloudFormation/GCP Config:**  
  - GCP Config (now Config Connector) can monitor resource properties, but it does not natively classify secrets in env vars.
  - CloudFormation is AWS-specific and not relevant here.

**Conclusion:** The approach is *mostly* automatable, but the final determination of "no secrets in env vars" may require manual review or advanced automation (e.g., DLP, regex, or policy-based checks).

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** **Score: 7/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - Listing and describing functions via `gcloud` is correct.
  - Checking for the presence of environment variables is correct.
  - Verifying Secret Manager API is enabled is correct.
  - **Missing:** No mention of how to programmatically determine if an environment variable contains a "secret" (e.g., pattern matching for "password", "secret", "key", or value entropy analysis).
  - **Missing:** No mention of how the function code actually *retrieves* secrets (e.g., via Secret Manager API calls or environment variable references).
  - **Missing:** No check for IAM permissions on Secret Manager or audit logs for secret access.
- **gcloud/CLI Methods:**  
  - All referenced commands are valid.
- **Manual Steps:**  
  - Not discussed, but some manual review is likely needed for ambiguous cases.
- **Program Name:**  
  - `gcp_functions_environment_variables_secrets_managed` is descriptive, but could be more specific (see Improvements).

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Detection of Secrets:**  
  - Specify how to identify secrets in environment variables. Consider using:
    - Regex patterns for variable names (e.g., `(?i)secret|password|token|key`)
    - Value entropy checks (for random-looking strings)
    - Integration with DLP API for more advanced detection
- **Verification of Secret Manager Usage:**  
  - Check if the function code references Secret Manager (e.g., via IAM permissions, audit logs, or code scanning if available).
  - Confirm that environment variables referencing secrets use the `projects/*/secrets/*` format (as per best practices for Secret Manager integration).
- **IAM/Audit Checks:**  
  - Optionally, verify that only authorized identities can access secrets in Secret Manager.
- **Program Name:**  
  - Suggest: `gcp_functions_envvars_no_plaintext_secrets` or `gcp_functions_envvars_secrets_via_secretmanager`
  - This is more explicit about the control being checked.
- **Documentation:**  
  - Add explicit documentation of limitations: "Automated checks may not catch all secrets; manual review may be required for ambiguous cases."

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- **Reason:**  
  The technical approach is mostly sound, but the detection of "secrets" in environment variables is not fully automatable and is not clearly addressed in the automation details. There is a risk of false negatives/positives without more advanced detection logic or manual review.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** MODIFY

- **What to Modify:**  
  - Clarify in the assessment that automated checks can *flag* potential secrets in environment variables, but manual review may be required for ambiguous cases.
  - Specify detection logic for secrets (e.g., regex, DLP).
  - Update the program name to be more explicit.
  - Optionally, add checks for Secret Manager usage patterns (e.g., environment variable values referencing Secret Manager, IAM permissions).
- **Re-classification:**  
  - Remain primarily Automated, but note the need for manual review in edge cases.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section             | Rating/Decision         | Notes                                                                                 |
|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation | PARTIALLY_AGREE        | Detection of secrets in env vars is not fully automatable.                            |
| Technical Accuracy  | 7/10                   | Valid commands, but lacks details on secret detection and Secret Manager usage checks. |
| Improvements        | See above              | Add detection logic, clarify limitations, update program name.                        |
| Confidence          | MEDIUM                 | Good base, but not fully robust without enhancements.                                 |
| Final Recommendation| MODIFY                  | Clarify limitations, add detection logic, update naming.                              |

---
